coredata.dc:title: Influence of in-situ and post-modification syntheses on the characteristic properties of tyrosine functionalized graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ravi, Pavithra Verthikere
Subramaniyam, Vinodhini
Pichumani, Moorthi
coredata.dc:description: Functionalization of GQDs is expected to enhance the optoelectronic properties which are beneficial to explore their applications in the field of semiconductors, sensors, electronics, etc., Tyrosine is a versatile zwitterionic aromatic amino acid, which could improve the optical and electronic properties of the pristine GQDs. The tyrosine functionalized GQDs are synthesized in two different methods via in-situ and post-modification methods to study their characteristics and are named them as IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs respectively. The structural, morphological, assembly, surface functional group, and optical characteristics are analyzed and compared to study the changes in the characteristics of the prepared functionalized GQDs. The characteristic properties of the IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs are contrasting and vary in size, morphology, absorbance wavelength, crystallinity, and emission behavior. The interesting result is that IS-Tyr-GQDs are crystalline fused spherical shape with 14–15 nm in size and PM-Tyr-GQDs are partially crystalline with cinctured-berry of 4–5 nm in size. Although the functional moiety is the same IS-Tyr-GQDs are excitation dependent with redshift whereas, PM-Tyr-GQDs are excitation independent with blue shift. AFM, HRTEM, XPS, XRD, and Raman spectroscopic analyses confirm the formation of two-dimensional (2D) Tyr-GQDs. Liquid Chromatography-Mass Spectra recorded evidence of the purity of the compound synthesized. Both IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs distinction in properties brings their diverse applications in bioimaging, biosensing, electronics, fabrication, and development of sensors.

head.ce:title: Influence of in-situ and post-modification syntheses on the characteristic properties of tyrosine functionalized graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Sri Ramakrishna Engineering College, Coimbatore 641 022, Tamilnadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Sri Ramakrishna Engineering College, Coimbatore – 641 022, Tamilnadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Functionalization of GQDs is expected to enhance the optoelectronic properties which are beneficial to explore their applications in the field of semiconductors, sensors, electronics, etc., Tyrosine is a versatile zwitterionic aromatic amino acid, which could improve the optical and electronic properties of the pristine GQDs. The tyrosine functionalized GQDs are synthesized in two different methods via in-situ and post-modification methods to study their characteristics and are named them as IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs respectively. The structural, morphological, assembly, surface functional group, and optical characteristics are analyzed and compared to study the changes in the characteristics of the prepared functionalized GQDs. The characteristic properties of the IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs are contrasting and vary in size, morphology, absorbance wavelength, crystallinity, and emission behavior. The interesting result is that IS-Tyr-GQDs are crystalline fused spherical shape with 14–15 nm in size and PM-Tyr-GQDs are partially crystalline with cinctured-berry of 4–5 nm in size. Although the functional moiety is the same IS-Tyr-GQDs are excitation dependent with redshift whereas, PM-Tyr-GQDs are excitation independent with blue shift. AFM, HRTEM, XPS, XRD, and Raman spectroscopic analyses confirm the formation of two-dimensional (2D) Tyr-GQDs. Liquid Chromatography-Mass Spectra recorded evidence of the purity of the compound synthesized. Both IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs distinction in properties brings their diverse applications in bioimaging, biosensing, electronics, fabrication, and development of sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: A prominent and top-ranked nanomaterial possessing chemical and physical stability, biocompatible, large surface-to-mass ratio, good aqueous dispersibility, fluorescence, and active functional sites on the surface is carbon-based nanomaterials namely graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [1–8]. Owing to their astonishing characteristics GQDs are footprinting in various domains right from biological science to mechanical applications [9]. Fluorescent carbon-based quantum dots are highly utilized in the field of biological science due to their multifunctionality, biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, enhanced optical properties, and many more [10,11]. Their electronic properties contribute greatly to the field of sensor development, solar cells, health care, disease diagnostics, therapies, and many more [6,12–22,106,107]. The GQDs synthesis includes various techniques (bottom-up and top-down), methods (pyrolysis, chemical oxidation, hydro/solvothermal, etc.) sources (organic waste, organic compounds, allotropes of carbon, etc.), and modifications (doping, surface functionalization, etc.) [4,11,23–32]. The choice of source, method, and functional moieties (hetero atoms, dyes, organic compounds, etc.) is important to consider during the synthesis of GQDs to fabricate them with desired properties.

Surface functionalization/modification/engineering of GQDs is believed to alter the optoelectronic properties of pristine GQDs [33]. The functional moieties include, metals [34–36], metal oxides [37,38], non-metals [32,39–42], biomolecules [43–47], dyes [48], amino acids [49–53] and many more. A variety of functionalized GQDs is used for developing immense and uncountable fluorescence sensors, electrochemical sensors, and bioimaging applications.

Functionalization of GQDs can be achieved by several methods including pre-and post-synthesis methods. In pre-synthesis or in-situ synthesis methods, desired functional group precursors are added directly to the reaction while the formation of nanomaterials. On the other hand, the majority of the surface functionalization is done by the post-modification synthesis method as this method gives full control over the quantity of the functional moiety added, and interaction duration between the GQDs and the functional moiety, reaction temperature, pH, etc., during the surface modifications. In-situ synthesis can either result in covalent functionalization, doping, or chemical adsorption. While post-modification synthesis methods can generally include covalent, noncovalent functionalization, grafting, and physical adsorption [54]. Differences between in-situ and post-modification synthesis methods are listed in Table 1 .

From a variety of functional moieties, amino acids are important and interesting biomolecules to functionalize with GQDs. The functional moiety of interest consists of different functional groups such as the carboxylic group, amine group, sulfur, aromatic, and aliphatic groups. The amphoteric and zwitterionic nature of amino acids makes them a potential candidate for functionalization [55]. From our previous review article, we observed that almost all the amino acids are used to functionalize carbon-based quantum dots (GQDs and CQDs). However, among the three aromatic amino acids (tryptophan, tyrosine, and phenylalanine), a fluorescent amino acid, tyrosine is not functionalized with either GQDs or CQDs [56]. Table 2 gives the list of amino acids used for functionalizing GQDs and the methods opted for synthesis and functionalization. From the table is it observed that the most commonly opted method of synthesis or functionalization is in-situ.

Our inquisitiveness to study the optoelectronic properties of tyrosine functionalized GQDs led us to come up with the present research. To study the properties of tyrosine functionalized GQDs (Tyr-GQDs), we opted for two methods namely, in-situ and post-modification methods of functionalization, and the resultant GQDs are named as IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs respectively. The morphological, surface, and optoelectronic properties are detailly studied and compared between the synthesized Tyr-GQDs.

The purpose of functionalizing amino acids with GQDs is to enhance the optoelectronic nature. Other than enhancing optoelectronic properties amino acids are a cost-effective, easily available source, and have excellent fluorescence. The presence of an amino group can react with the carboxylic group of GQDs to form an amide bond [77]. On the amide group, the introduction of other groups leads to the enhancement of fluorescence and increment fluorescence lifetime [78,79].

The idiosyncratic properties of the IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs exhibit disparity in size, morphology, absorbance wavelength, crystallinity, and emission behavior. The resultant IS-Tyr-GQDs are crystalline fused spherical with 14–15 nm in size and PM-Tyr-GQDs are partially crystalline with cinctured-berry of 4–5 nm in size. The formation of two-dimensional (2D) Tyr-GQDs, composition, and their morphology is confirmed with the help of AFM, HRTEM, XPS, XRD, and Raman spectroscopic analyses. IS-Tyr-GQDs are excitation dependant with redshift while, PM-Tyr-GQDs are excitation independent with blue shift. Both IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs distinction in properties bring their diverse applications in bioimaging, biosensing, electronics, health care, environmental monitoring systems, fabrication, and development of sensors.

Analytical grade reagents and solvents are used for the experimental analysis and used as it is, without any further purification. Deionized water from the Millipore – Merck is used throughout the experiments for synthesis and washing. Chemicals such as Citric acid, L-tyrosine, were provided by Sigma-Aldrich, India. UV–Visible absorption and photoluminescent emission experiments were recorded on the Agilent Technologies Spectro fluorophotometer (G9800A) at 298 K. Bruker ATR-FTIR spectroscopy (Bruker Alpha – T, Germany) is used to measure infrared spectroscopy. JEOL JEM 2100 High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) instrument is used to analyze the structure and morphology. All the experiments were repeated three times under similar experimental conditions for checking the repeatability and uncertainty. X-ray diffraction measurements are carried out using Cu Ka radiation as an X-ray source (λ - 1.542 A°) (X'pert PRO PANalytical instrument, The Netherlands). FE-SEM - EDAX (SIGMA HV–Carl Zeiss with Bruker Quantax 200–Z10 EDS Detector). XPS measurements are carried out using the ULVAC-PHI, PHI5000 Version Probe III. Fluorescence lifetime values are analyzed by JOBIN-VYON M/S. with the LED's 390 nm. Laser Raman analysis is done using HORIBA, LabRAM HR Evolution, Laser 532 nm. Liquid Chromatography- Mass Spectra (LC-MS/MS) 8045 Shimadzu is used to record LC-Ms spectra. AFM images are recorded by Multimode Scanning Probe Microscopy (AFM/STM) NTMDT.
Tyr-GQDs were prepared by two different methods namely, in-situ synthesis and the post-modification method. The thermal pyrolysis method is used for both the above-mentioned techniques.

Citric acid with the equal molar ration of L-tyrosine is measured and taken together in a 100 mL beaker and mixed. The beaker is heated at approximately 200 °C. Initially, the mixture slowly melts and slowly changes its color to dark brown after 25 min. At this stage, 20 mL of deionized water is added drop by drop under vigorous stirring at a rate of 500 rpm to dissolve the formed Tyr-GQDs in an aqueous medium. The concentration of the resultant dispersion is calculated and used as a stock solution.

In the first step, only citric acid is taken in a 100 mL beaker and heated at 200 °C. The citric slowly melts and changes its color and to the molten citric acid, 10 mL of deionized water is added drop by drop under vigorous stirring at a rate of 500 rpm to synthesize GQDs as reported in [80]. L-tyrosine is insoluble in water, hence to dissolve few drops of concentrated HCl are added. The L-tyrosine solution is made up to 10 mL and slowly added to the GQDs dispersion and allowed to stir continuously for 2 h. The concentration of the resultant solution is calculated and used as a stock solution.
Furthermore, pure IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs are freeze-dried at −45 °C to obtain solid Tyr-GQDs powder for further characterizations.

Fig. 1 is the schematic representation of the formation mechanism of Is-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs via in-situ and post-modification synthesis and functionalization respectively. The IS-Tyr-GQDs involve a high degree of carbonization as both citric acid and Tyr undergo thermal pyrolysis hence, a low degree of surface oxidation. While, in the case of PM-Tyr-GQDs, only citric acid undergoes carbonization therefore, a high degree of surface oxidation and low carbonization is observed.

The excitation and emission wavelength of IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs are tested at the same concentration of 0.0001 M (Fig. 2 ) to investigate their absorbance and emission wavelength, emission behavior (excitation dependent or independent), bandgap, etc., Surprisingly, there is a remarkable change in the absorption and emission wavelength of the IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs. The absorption wavelength of IS-Tyr-GQDs is 409 nm and its emission wavelength is observed at 494 nm. Whereas for PM-Tyr-GQDs, the observed absorption wavelength is 394 nm and its emission wavelength is 479 nm (Fig. 2). The calculated band gap for the Is-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs is found to be 3.03 and 3.15 eV which is in agreement with the reported values [81]. The increased surface oxidation noticed in PM-Tyr-GQDs must be responsible for the blue shift in emission wavelength [33] but, the observed result is contradictory when compared with the emission wavelength of IS-Tyr-GQDs. The possible reason for the redshift instead of the blue shift of IS-Tyr-GQDs is likely due to the nitrogen content from tyrosine. However, the increased size of IS-Tyr-GQDs also contributes to the redshift in wavelength.

During the in-situ carbonization process, the nitrogen atom is capable of binding along with the carbon atom either in the carbon core or on the surfaces as an amide group. The binding may result as both C and N atoms possess a similar electronic structure and atomic radius. The probability of these groups to undergo a transition from the ground state to the excited singlet state of electrons, thus causing surface defect which is responsible for increased emission wavelength [82]. Fig. 3 EDX spectrum confirms that IS-Tyr-GQDs own an increased percentage of N when paralleled with PM-Tyr-GQDs. It is interesting to note that, although the functional moiety is the same, based on the method of functionalization and resultant size, IS-Tyr-GQDs exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence emission property and redshift in wavelength, and PM-Tyr-GQDs are excitation independent is shown in Fig. 4
[83]. The extended analysis of fluorescence dependency on the excitation wavelength is also added in the SI as Fig. SI 1.

To further investigate the PL characteristics in detail, fluorescence quantum yield (ФF) and the lifetime of the excited molecules were analyzed. The formula used for the quantum yield calculations is given in the supporting information. The quantum yield of IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs calculated concerning quinine sulfate are 0.50 and 0.51 respectively [84,85]. From the Time-Correlated Single-Photon Counting (TCSPC) analysis, the measured lifetime values of IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs are 4.12 and 5.36 ns respectively (Fig. 5 ). The relative amplitude, lifetime, formula used for average lifetime calculation, and average lifetime values of GQDs and Tyr-GQDs are tabulated in SI as Table SI 1 along with the decay profile of pristine GQDs (Fig. SI 2). Both Tyr-GQDs are best fitted with triexponential decay patterns [86] whereas pristine GQDs are fitted with biexponential decay profiles.

FTIR analysis of IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs confirms the presence of different functional groups present (Fig. 6 ). The vibrational band at 3343 cm−1 confirms the presence of the -N-H group [87]. The presence of CC is confirmed at 1650 cm−1 in pristine GQDs which is absent in the Tyr-GQDs and a new band at 1633 cm−1 corresponding to the NH stretching is noticed [87]. The vibrational band for CN is found at 1518 and 1238 cm−1 in both the Tyr-GQDs. In addition to this, in IS-Tyr-GQDs, the carboxylic CO band is found at 1711 cm−1, which is absent in PM-Tyr-GQDs [87,88]. The CH stretching band of graphene is noticed at 1390 cm−1. These additional bands confirm that in IS-Tyr-GQDs, tyrosine and citric acid are mutually carbonized and fabricated as represented in Fig. 1. Whereas in PM-Tyr-GQDs, these functional groups of GQDs are surrounded by the tyrosine molecule creating a cinctured-berry structure which is in concurrence with the HR-TEM results.

FESEM images of the PM-Tyr-GQDs are shown in Fig. SI-3 for different length scale ranges from μm to nm. The coral reef-like structure in post-modification incorporation of GQDs with L-tyrosine might be crystalline. IS-Tyr-GQDs survey spectrum confirms the presence of C, N, and O (Fig. 7 a). The presence of CO (288.8 and 532.6 eV), CC (284.7 eV), CN (285.9 and 400.5 eV), are confirmed in the high-resolution spectra of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen (Fig. 7b–d) [67,89]. PM-Tyr-GQDs survey spectrum confirms the presence of C, N, and O (Fig. 8 a). The presence of CO (289 eV and 533 eV), CC (284.7 eV), COH (285.4 eV), NH (401.7 eV) are confirmed in the high-resolution spectra of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen (Fig. 8b–d). Additionally, peaks with considerable intensity at 286.4 and 287 eV are corresponds to the presence of CO and COC, respectively [90]. The N/C ratio of PM-Tyr-GQDs (0.092) is found to be higher than the IS-Tyr-GQDs (0.047) calculated by XPS analysis due to the post-modification method of synthesis which is in good harmony with the formation mechanism of PM-Tyr-GQDs as illustrated in Fig. 1.

The information related to the size, morphology, and crystallinity of the IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs is studied using HR-TEM and XRD. The HR-TEM images of the bare GQDs are given (Fig. SI 4) which depict their size range of 5–10 nm. To investigate the morphology of the Tyr-GQDs as a state of the sample we carried out an HRTEM analysis for the as-prepared Tyr-GQDs by coating the liquid samples on the copper grid. Fig. 9 gives the morphological view of both the Tyr-GQDs in different nanoscale (a & e:100; b & f: 50; c & g: 20 and d & h: 10 nm). In comparing IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs, in a liquid state, we observed a major change in the morphology, arrangement of quantum dots, size, crystallinity, and stability. The initial and swift analysis of HR-TEM reveals the morphology changes. The IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs are spherical with an average diameter of 14–15 and 4–5 nm respectively. Additionally, HRTEM images of Tyr-GQDs of various magnifications are given in Figs. SI 5 and 6. For the marked spots in Fig. 10 a and b, calculated d-spacing is tabulated in the SI as Tables 2 and 3 . The expected morphology for PM-Tyr-GQDs is GQDs surrounded by tyrosine molecule, however surprisingly we notice that the tyrosine molecule is surrounded by GQDs which is evidenced by comparing the size of pristine GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs. Furthermore, the size of both the GQDs analyzed from the HR-TEM results is in harmony with the absorbance results. As the size of GQDs increases, the absorbance wavelength shifts towards the higher wavelength region and vice-versa, which is factual in the case of IS-Tyr-GQDs (redshift: ~20 nm) and PM-Tyr-GQDs (blue shift). On studying the HR-TEM images, and in detail, it is confirmed that the IS-Tyr-GQDs are highly crystalline with the lattice spacing of 0.25 nm [91], and (1120) plane of graphitic structure [76]. Table 2 gives the overall comparison and characteristic changes between the IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs. For our curiosity to study morphology and to evidence the formed nanostructures are Tyr-GQDs and are not fluorescent oligomers, the liquid suspension of pure IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-yr-GQDs are freeze-dried at −45 °C. The freeze-dried samples are once again subjected to HRTEM analysis and the images in various nanoscale magnifications (a & e:100; b & f: 50; c & g: 10 and d & h: 0.5 nm) are provided in the (Fig. 11 ). HRTEM results infer the 2D structure and (1120) graphitic plane of GQDs are confirmed with the lattice fringes of 0.25 nm and 0.21 nm respectively for IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-yr-GQDs [76,92–94]. It is interesting to note that, the arrangements of atoms in solid form are highly ordered may be due to the changes in the alignment of the atoms and the functional groups during the process of freeze-drying which generates a variety of stresses and has an impact on the stability of nanostructures, crystallization, pH, interfacial effects, etc., [95]. The particle size histogram for the solid IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs is provided in Fig. SI 7.

AFM analysis of Tyr-GQDs (Fig. 12 ) illustrates the surface morphology along with the thickness of freeze-dried samples. The 3D profile of IS-Tyr-GQDs (Fig. 12a) possesses morphology similar to that of earlier literature reported for GQDs with 1–3 nm thickness and average roughness value of 0.507 nm due to the in-situ method of synthesis [96]. Whereas, the PM-Tyr-GQDs (Fig. 12b) have identical morphology to functionalized GQDs on the surface which is owing to the post-modification method of synthesis where dissolved tyrosine is added to the synthesized GQDs. The average thickness and roughness of PM-Tyr-GQDs are found to be 4–5 nm and 1.152 nm respectively. The increase in average roughness is indicative of the presence of tyrosine on the edges of GQDs [97,98] which is a significant characteristic of PM-Tyr-GQDs.

From Fig. 13 , it is clear that both IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs possess the graphitic plane of (002) centered at 22.7 and 22.53 degrees respectively [2]. The calculated interplanar distance (d) for both Tyr-GQDs is increased (0.39 nm) on compared with the pristine GQDs (0.37 nm). The increased lattice space is induced by the amino functionalization which has the potency over the crystalline nature of the Tyr-GQDs (partially crystalline) which is comparable with the pristine GQDs [99]. The synthesized Tyr-GQDs possess both (1120) and (002) planes of graphene which is confirmed from HRTEM and XRD respectively.

Laser Raman spectra of Tyr-GQDs are analyzed in a liquid state with the laser source of 532 nm. Raman spectra of Tyr-GQDs reveal the presence of D and G bands, at 1328 and 1569 cm−1 respectively (Fig. 14 ). The ratio of D and G band intensities for IS-Try-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs are found to be >1. Interestingly, a new band at 1442 cm−1 appeared which corresponds to the organic CC asymmetric stretch of -CH2 and -CH3 of either graphene or tyrosine moiety [100]. The reason for the sample frequencies and intensity increase of IS-Tyr-GQDs is due to the increased size when compared to that of PM-Tyr-GQDs increases [101].

Liquid Chromatography-Mass Spectra (LC-MS) is recorded by electro spraying the solution of surfactant less pristine GQDs (synthesized as reported in our earlier literature [102]), IS-Tyr-GQDs, and PM-Tyr-GQDs. The formation of GQDs and functionalization of tyrosine with GQDs have been confirmed with the LC-MS spectra obtained. The LC-MS spectra of GQDs (Fig. 15 a) have major fragmentation peaks at 129, 147, and 194 m/z values. Two fragment ion m/z 147 and 123 correspond to the loss of H2O and CH2CO molecules. The peak at m/z 194 corresponds to the C11H14O3 + H+ of graphene moiety. The other minor peaks associated with the fragments of GQDs (Table SI 4) are also similar to the electron spray ionization (ESI) mass spectra recorded by Xiaoyun He et al. [103] (Table SI 5). For IS-Tyr-GQDs we noticed m/z peaks at 129, 147, 161, and 193 which is similar to that of GQDs (Fig. 15b). The slight variation in m/z values may be due to the presence of N of tyrosine moiety, and the cleavage of the CN bond. The presence of CN bond in IS-Tyr-GQDs is confirmed by both FTIR and XPS analyses. The nitrogen infusion into the carbon ring in and as a ring member is due to the carbonization process and followed by the formation of corresponding IS-Tyr-GQDs [104]. The ESI spectra of PM-Tyr-GQDs (Fig. 15c) confirm the functionalization of tyrosine with GQDs as it consists of a combination of fragmentation peaks of both GQDs and tyrosine. In PM-Tyr-GQDs, the m/z peak at 147 resembling the loss of H2O and CH2CO molecules (as in pristine GQDs and IS-Tyr-GQDs) are present. The loss of 2 neutral fragments of CO from the fragment ion at m/z 147 resulted in the two fragments ion at m/z 119 and 91. High collision energy fragment ions at m/z 123 dissociate to form a fragment of m/z 95 with loss of CO followed by m/z 65 (Fig. 15d). Loss of H2O and CO from protonated tyrosine fragment ion is observed at 136 m/z value [105].

Comparing the synthesized Tyr-GQDs via in-situ and post-modification methods confirmed that the method of functionalization greatly influences the properties of Tyr-GQDs by the arrangement of the functional groups around GQDs and by the nature of the interaction. The degree of carbonization and oxidation are inversely proportional. From the EDX analysis Fig. 3, it is clear that the percentage of carbon is high (73.94 %) for IS-Tyr-GQDs and low (56.28 %) for PM-Tyr-GQDs which describes that the degree of carbonization for IS-Tyr-GQDs is high and surface oxidation is less. The contrary is observed for PM-Tyr-GQDs where surface oxidation is high and the degree of carbonization is less. The differences between the characteristics of the IS-Tyr-GQDs and PM-Tyr-GQDs are listed in Table 3.

L-Tyrosine functionalized GQDs are prepared using in-situ and post-modification methods. Solid Tyr-GQDs are obtained in powder form through freeze drying the liquid sample. The detailed comparison between the synthesized Tyr-GQDs reveals contrasting properties. It is intesting to note that the state of the Tyr-GQDs also play important role in assembling the atoms and functional groups on the surface of the GQDs which is responsible for the changes in the optoelectronic, morphological and crystalline nature of the material. The purity of the products formed is examined by mass spectra and the 2D structure of graphene moiety is confirmed by AFM, HRTEM, XPS, and Raman spectral analyses. Right from size to the crystalline behavior, they exhibit variations, for instance, the morphology of the IS-Tyr-GQDs has diverged from PM-Tyr-GQDs. The IS-Tyr-GQDs possess a fused-spherical structure with an increased average particle size of approximately 14–15 nm. But, the PM-Tyr-GQDs have formed a cinctured-berry structure with a decreased average particle size of approximately 4–5 nm. The emission of IS-Tyr-GQDs is depending on the wavelength of excitation due to the change in crystallinity. The increment in size and excitation dependence nature caused a red-shift in emission wavelength of the IS-Tyr-GQDs. Whereas, the emission of PM-Tyr-GQDs is independent of the excitation wavelength since there is no change in crystallinity. The decrement in size and excitation-independent nature caused a blue shift in the emission wavelength of the PM-Tyr-GQDs. The fluorescence properties of Tyr-GQDs are further confirmed by their fluorescence lifetime (IS-Tyr-GQDs: 4.12 ns:: PM-Tyr-GQDs: 5.36 ns), and quantum yield measurements (IS-Tyr-GQDs: 0.50:: PM-Tyr-GQDs: 0.51). The formation of PM-Tyr-GQDs has been confirmed from XPS and possesses a coral reef-like structure when imaged using FESEM. The current research findings open up a new perspective to re-think and design the methodology to functionalize GQDs and apply them as a potential candidate for various sensor fabrications, healthcare and environment monitoring, and bioimaging agents.
